notes.heading: Introduction to Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
notes.text: This chapter deals with organic compounds containing the carbonyl group, >C=O. Aldehydes and ketones are called carbonyl compounds. Carboxylic acids also contain a carbonyl group, but they have an additional hydroxyl group attached to the same carbon.
points: The carbonyl group is >C=O, where carbon is double bonded to oxygen.
Aldehydes contain the functional group –CHO, called the formyl group.
General formula of aldehydes: R–CHO.
Examples of aldehydes: CH3CHO is ethanal; C6H5CHO is benzaldehyde.
In aldehydes, the carbonyl carbon is attached to one hydrogen atom and one alkyl or aryl group.
Ketones contain the carbonyl group between two carbon groups.
General formula of ketones: R–CO–R'.
Examples of ketones: CH3COCH3 is propanone or acetone; CH3COC2H5 is butanone.
In ketones, the carbonyl carbon is attached to two alkyl or aryl groups.
Carboxylic acids contain the functional group –COOH, called the carboxyl group.
The carboxyl group is made of a carbonyl group (>C=O) and a hydroxyl group (–OH).
General formula of carboxylic acids: R–COOH.
Examples of carboxylic acids: CH3COOH is ethanoic acid or acetic acid; HCOOH is methanoic acid or formic acid; C6H5COOH is benzoic acid.
Simple difference: aldehyde = R–CHO, ketone = R–CO–R', carboxylic acid = R–COOH.
Memory trick: aldehyde has carbonyl group at the end of the chain; ketone has carbonyl group in the middle; carboxylic acid has carbonyl group plus –OH.

notes.heading: Nomenclature of Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
notes.text: Nomenclature means naming of compounds. In this chapter, common names and IUPAC names are important.
points: Aldehydes have the functional group –CHO.
In IUPAC naming of aldehydes, the suffix is –al.
The longest carbon chain containing the –CHO group is selected.
The carbon of –CHO is always counted as carbon number 1.
HCHO: common name formaldehyde; IUPAC name methanal.
CH3CHO: common name acetaldehyde; IUPAC name ethanal.
CH3CH2CHO: common name propionaldehyde; IUPAC name propanal.
CH3CH2CH2CHO: common name butyraldehyde; IUPAC name butanal.
Example: CH3CH2CH(CH3)CHO is 2-methylbutanal because the aldehyde carbon is C1.
Ketones have the functional group >C=O.
In IUPAC naming of ketones, the suffix is –one.
The longest carbon chain containing the carbonyl group is selected.
Numbering is done so that the carbonyl carbon gets the lowest possible number.
CH3COCH3: common name acetone; IUPAC name propanone.
CH3COCH2CH3: common name ethyl methyl ketone; IUPAC name butan-2-one.
CH3CH2COCH2CH3: IUPAC name pentan-3-one.
CH3COCH2CH2CH3: IUPAC name pentan-2-one.
Carboxylic acids contain the functional group –COOH.
In IUPAC naming of carboxylic acids, the suffix is –oic acid.
The carbon of –COOH is always carbon number 1.
HCOOH: common name formic acid; IUPAC name methanoic acid.
CH3COOH: common name acetic acid; IUPAC name ethanoic acid.
CH3CH2COOH: common name propionic acid; IUPAC name propanoic acid.
CH3CH2CH2COOH: common name butyric acid; IUPAC name butanoic acid.
C6H5CHO is benzaldehyde.
C6H5COCH3 is acetophenone; IUPAC name 1-phenylethanone.
C6H5COC6H5 is benzophenone.
C6H5COOH is benzoic acid.
Memory trick: aldehyde = –al, ketone = –one, carboxylic acid = –oic acid.

notes.heading: Structure of the Carbonyl Group
notes.text: Aldehydes and ketones contain the carbonyl group >C=O. Carboxylic acids also contain a carbonyl group as part of –COOH. The structure and polarity of the carbonyl group control many reactions.
points: The carbonyl group is C=O.
The C=O double bond consists of one sigma bond and one pi bond: C=O = 1 sigma + 1 pi.
The carbonyl carbon is sp2 hybridised.
Example: in CH3CHO, the carbonyl carbon is attached to oxygen, hydrogen and the carbon of CH3, so it has three regions of electron density and is sp2 hybridised.
The shape around carbonyl carbon is trigonal planar.
The bond angle around carbonyl carbon is approximately 120°.
Oxygen is more electronegative than carbon, so it pulls shared electrons towards itself.
In the carbonyl group, oxygen has partial negative charge and carbon has partial positive charge: Cδ+=Oδ−.
Because carbonyl carbon has partial positive charge, it is electron deficient and is attacked by nucleophiles.
Examples of nucleophiles: CN−, OH−, NH3.
Carbonyl compounds mainly show nucleophilic addition reactions.
Resonance representation: R2C=O ↔ R2C+–O−.
Aldehydes and ketones are reactive because their carbonyl carbon is electron deficient.
Aldehydes are generally more reactive than ketones.
In aldehydes, the carbonyl carbon is attached to one alkyl group and one hydrogen atom.
In ketones, the carbonyl carbon is attached to two alkyl groups.
Alkyl groups show +I effect and push electrons towards carbonyl carbon, reducing its positive charge.
Ketones are less reactive than aldehydes due to greater +I effect and steric hindrance.
Aldehydes have one small hydrogen atom near the carbonyl carbon, so nucleophilic attack is easier.
Ketones have two alkyl or aryl groups, creating more crowding near the carbonyl carbon.
Reactivity order for nucleophilic addition: HCHO > RCHO > RCOCH3 > RCOR'.
Important NCERT points: carbonyl carbon is sp2 hybridised, trigonal planar, bond angle about 120°, and main reaction is nucleophilic addition.

notes.heading: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
notes.text: Aldehydes and ketones are prepared mainly by oxidation, dehydrogenation, ozonolysis, hydration of alkynes, and special reactions such as Rosenmund reduction, Stephen reaction, Etard reaction and Gattermann-Koch reaction.
points: Primary alcohols on controlled oxidation give aldehydes: RCH2OH → RCHO.
Example: CH3CH2OH → CH3CHO. Ethanol gives ethanal.
If oxidation is strong, aldehydes further change into carboxylic acids: CH3CHO → CH3COOH.
Secondary alcohols on oxidation give ketones: R–CHOH–R' → R–CO–R'.
Example: CH3CHOHCH3 → CH3COCH3. Propan-2-ol gives propanone.
Dehydrogenation means removal of hydrogen.
Alcohol vapours passed over heated copper at 573 K undergo dehydrogenation.
Primary alcohol gives aldehyde: RCH2OH —Cu, 573 K→ RCHO + H2.
Example: CH3CH2OH —Cu, 573 K→ CH3CHO + H2.
Secondary alcohol gives ketone: RCHOHR' —Cu, 573 K→ RCOR' + H2.
Example: CH3CHOHCH3 —Cu, 573 K→ CH3COCH3 + H2.
Ozonolysis of alkenes gives aldehydes or ketones after reduction with zinc dust and water.
General ozonolysis: Alkene —O3→ ozonide —Zn/H2O→ aldehyde/ketone.
Example: CH2=CH2 —O3/Zn,H2O→ 2HCHO. Ethene gives methanal.
Example: CH3CH=CH2 —O3/Zn,H2O→ CH3CHO + HCHO. Propene gives ethanal and methanal.
Example: (CH3)2C=CH2 —O3/Zn,H2O→ CH3COCH3 + HCHO.
Hydration of alkynes occurs with H2O in the presence of HgSO4/H2SO4.
Ethyne gives ethanal: HC≡CH —H2O, HgSO4/H2SO4→ CH3CHO.
Other alkynes generally give ketones.
Example: CH3C≡CH —H2O, HgSO4/H2SO4→ CH3COCH3. Propyne gives propanone.
Rosenmund reduction converts acid chlorides to aldehydes: RCOCl —H2/Pd-BaSO4→ RCHO.
Pd-BaSO4 is a poisoned catalyst in Rosenmund reduction.
Example: CH3COCl —H2/Pd-BaSO4→ CH3CHO. Acetyl chloride gives ethanal.
Stephen reaction converts nitriles to aldehydes: RCN —SnCl2/HCl→ RCH=NH —H2O→ RCHO.
Example: CH3CN —SnCl2/HCl→ CH3CH=NH —H2O→ CH3CHO.
Etard reaction: toluene is oxidised by chromyl chloride to benzaldehyde: C6H5CH3 —CrO2Cl2→ C6H5CHO.
Gattermann-Koch reaction: benzene reacts with CO and HCl in the presence of AlCl3/CuCl to give benzaldehyde: C6H6 + CO + HCl —AlCl3/CuCl→ C6H5CHO.
Ketones are prepared from acid chlorides using dialkyl cadmium: 2RCOCl + R'2Cd → 2RCOR' + CdCl2.
Example: 2CH3COCl + (CH3)2Cd → 2CH3COCH3 + CdCl2.
Ketones are prepared from nitriles using Grignard reagent followed by hydrolysis: RCN + R'MgX —H3O+→ RCOR'.
Example: CH3CN + CH3MgBr —H3O+→ CH3COCH3.
Memory trick: 1° alcohol gives aldehyde; 2° alcohol gives ketone; acid chloride gives aldehyde by Rosenmund reduction; nitrile gives aldehyde by Stephen reaction.

notes.heading: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
notes.text: Aldehydes and ketones are polar carbonyl compounds. Their physical properties depend on polarity, molecular size and hydrogen bonding.
points: Lower aldehydes and ketones are gases or volatile liquids.
Higher aldehydes and ketones are liquids or solids.
Methanal (HCHO) is a gas.
Ethanal (CH3CHO) is a volatile liquid.
Propanone or acetone (CH3COCH3) is a liquid.
Many aldehydes and ketones have pleasant smell and are used in perfumes and flavouring agents.
Benzaldehyde (C6H5CHO) has almond-like smell.
Vanillin is used as a flavouring agent.
Cinnamaldehyde gives the smell of cinnamon.
The carbonyl group is polar because oxygen is more electronegative than carbon: Cδ+=Oδ−.
Aldehydes and ketones have dipole-dipole attractions between molecules.
Their boiling points are higher than hydrocarbons and ethers of comparable molecular mass due to polarity.
Their boiling points are lower than alcohols of comparable molecular mass because alcohols form intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
Boiling point order for similar molecular masses: alcohols > aldehydes/ketones > ethers/hydrocarbons.
Aldehydes and ketones do not form hydrogen bonds with themselves because they do not have O–H or N–H bonds.
Aldehydes and ketones can form hydrogen bonds with water because carbonyl oxygen accepts hydrogen bonds from water.
Lower aldehydes and ketones are soluble in water due to hydrogen bonding with water.
Examples of water-soluble lower members: HCHO, CH3CHO, CH3COCH3.
Hydrogen bonding with water may be represented as >C=O···H–O–H.
Solubility in water decreases as the size of the alkyl group increases.
Large alkyl groups are hydrophobic and reduce water solubility.
Aldehydes and ketones are generally soluble in organic solvents such as benzene, ether and alcohol.
Important comparison: aldehydes and ketones are polar, do not show intermolecular hydrogen bonding with themselves, can form hydrogen bonds with water, and lower members are water-soluble.

notes.heading: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
notes.text: Aldehydes and ketones show important reactions because the carbonyl group is polar. The carbonyl carbon is electron deficient and is attacked by nucleophiles.
points: The carbonyl group is polar: Cδ+=Oδ−.
Main reactions of aldehydes and ketones include nucleophilic addition, reduction, oxidation, reactions due to alpha hydrogen, and identification tests.
General nucleophilic addition: R2C=O + Nu− → R2C(OH)Nu.
Examples of nucleophiles: CN−, HSO3−, NH2OH, NH2NH2, RMgX.
Aldehydes and ketones react with HCN to form cyanohydrins: RCHO + HCN → RCH(OH)CN.
Example: CH3CHO + HCN → CH3CH(OH)CN.
For ketones: CH3COCH3 + HCN → (CH3)2C(OH)CN.
Aldehydes and ketones react with sodium hydrogen sulphite, NaHSO3, to form crystalline addition products.
General reaction: RCHO + NaHSO3 → RCH(OH)SO3Na.
NaHSO3 addition is useful for purification and separation of aldehydes and ketones.
Grignard reagents, RMgX, add to aldehydes and ketones; hydrolysis gives alcohols.
Formaldehyde gives primary alcohol: HCHO + RMgX —H3O+→ RCH2OH.
Aldehydes give secondary alcohols: RCHO + R'MgX —H3O+→ RCH(OH)R'.
Ketones give tertiary alcohols: RCOR' + R''MgX —H3O+→ RC(OH)(R')R''.
Aldehydes and ketones react with ammonia derivatives such as NH2OH, NH2NH2, C6H5NHNH2 and NH2CONHNH2.
With hydroxylamine: R2C=O + NH2OH → R2C=NOH + H2O. Product is oxime.
With hydrazine: R2C=O + NH2NH2 → R2C=NNH2 + H2O. Product is hydrazone.
With phenylhydrazine: R2C=O + C6H5NHNH2 → R2C=NNHC6H5 + H2O. Product is phenylhydrazone.
With 2,4-DNP, aldehydes and ketones give yellow, orange or red precipitate of 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone.
Aldehydes are reduced to primary alcohols: RCHO —[H]→ RCH2OH.
Example: CH3CHO —[H]→ CH3CH2OH.
Ketones are reduced to secondary alcohols: RCOR' —[H]→ RCHOHR'.
Example: CH3COCH3 —[H]→ CH3CHOHCH3.
Common reducing agents: LiAlH4, NaBH4 and H2/Ni.
Clemmensen reduction reduces aldehydes and ketones to hydrocarbons using Zn-Hg/HCl: R2C=O → R2CH2.
Example: CH3COCH3 —Zn-Hg/HCl→ CH3CH2CH3.
Wolff-Kishner reduction reduces aldehydes and ketones to hydrocarbons using NH2NH2/KOH and heat: R2C=O → R2CH2.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised to carboxylic acids: RCHO —[O]→ RCOOH.
Example: CH3CHO —[O]→ CH3COOH.
Common oxidising agents for aldehydes: KMnO4, K2Cr2O7/H+, Tollens' reagent and Fehling's solution.
Ketones are difficult to oxidise because they do not have hydrogen attached to carbonyl carbon.
Aldehydes reduce Tollens' reagent and give a silver mirror.
Aliphatic aldehydes reduce Fehling's solution and give red-brown precipitate of Cu2O.
Alpha hydrogen means hydrogen attached to the carbon next to the carbonyl carbon.
Compounds having alpha hydrogen can show aldol condensation and haloform reaction.
Aldol condensation example: 2CH3CHO —dil. NaOH→ CH3CH(OH)CH2CHO.
On heating, aldol loses water: CH3CH(OH)CH2CHO —Δ→ CH3CH=CHCHO + H2O.
Compounds containing CH3CO– group show haloform reaction.
With iodine and alkali, they give yellow precipitate of iodoform, CHI3.
Example: CH3COCH3 —I2/NaOH→ CHI3.
Reactivity order toward nucleophilic addition: HCHO > RCHO > RCOR'.
Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones because they have less steric hindrance and less +I effect.

notes.heading: Nucleophilic Addition Reactions in Detail
notes.text: Aldehydes and ketones mainly undergo nucleophilic addition reactions due to the polar carbonyl group. The nucleophile attacks the partially positive carbonyl carbon.
points: A nucleophile is an electron-rich species.
Examples of nucleophiles: CN−, OH−, H− and RMgX.
The carbonyl carbon is partially positive: Cδ+=Oδ−.
General mechanism step 1: R2C=O + Nu− → R2C(O−)Nu.
In step 1, the nucleophile attacks the carbonyl carbon and electrons of the double bond shift to oxygen.
General mechanism step 2: R2C(O−)Nu + H+ → R2C(OH)Nu.
In step 2, negatively charged oxygen accepts a proton and forms alcohol.
The final addition product has –OH and Nu on the same carbon.
Addition of HCN gives cyanohydrins: R2C=O + HCN → R2C(OH)CN.
Cyanohydrin contains both –OH and –CN groups.
Example with ethanal: CH3CHO + HCN → CH3CH(OH)CN; product is 2-hydroxypropanenitrile.
Example with propanone: CH3COCH3 + HCN → (CH3)2C(OH)CN; product is 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropanenitrile.
Pure HCN is a weak acid and gives very little CN−.
The HCN addition reaction is carried out in presence of base such as NaOH or KCN to provide CN−.
Aldehydes and ketones react with sodium hydrogen sulphite, NaHSO3, to form crystalline addition compounds.
General reaction for aldehydes: RCHO + NaHSO3 → RCH(OH)SO3Na.
General reaction for ketones: R2C=O + NaHSO3 → R2C(OH)SO3Na.
NaHSO3 addition products are useful for separation and purification of aldehydes and ketones.
Grignard reagent has general formula RMgX.
Examples of Grignard reagents: CH3MgBr and C2H5MgBr.
Grignard reagent behaves like a source of R− and attacks carbonyl carbon.
After hydrolysis, Grignard addition gives alcohols.
Formaldehyde gives primary alcohol: HCHO + RMgX → RCH2OMgX; RCH2OMgX —H3O+→ RCH2OH.
Example: HCHO + CH3MgBr —H3O+→ CH3CH2OH.
Aldehydes give secondary alcohols: RCHO + R'MgX —H3O+→ RCH(OH)R'.
Example: CH3CHO + CH3MgBr —H3O+→ CH3CH(OH)CH3.
Ketones give tertiary alcohols: RCOR' + R''MgX —H3O+→ RC(OH)(R')R''.
Example: CH3COCH3 + CH3MgBr —H3O+→ (CH3)3COH.
Aldehydes react with alcohols in dry HCl gas to form hemiacetals and acetals.
Hemiacetal formation: RCHO + R'OH → RCH(OH)OR'.
Acetal formation: RCH(OH)OR' + R'OH → RCH(OR')2 + H2O.
Ketones react with alcohols to form hemiketals and ketals.
Aldehydes and ketones react with ammonia derivatives of general formula H2N–Z.
General reaction: R2C=O + H2N–Z → R2C=N–Z + H2O.
This is a condensation reaction because water is removed.
With hydroxylamine (NH2OH), product is oxime: R2C=O + NH2OH → R2C=NOH + H2O.
With hydrazine (NH2NH2), product is hydrazone: R2C=O + NH2NH2 → R2C=NNH2 + H2O.
With phenylhydrazine (C6H5NHNH2), product is phenylhydrazone: R2C=O + C6H5NHNH2 → R2C=NNHC6H5 + H2O.
With 2,4-DNP, product is 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone and yellow/orange/red precipitate is observed.
With semicarbazide (NH2CONHNH2), product is semicarbazone: R2C=O + NH2CONHNH2 → R2C=NNHCONH2 + H2O.
Reactivity order in nucleophilic addition: HCHO > RCHO > RCOR'.
Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones due to electronic effect and steric effect.
In ketones, two alkyl groups donate electrons by +I effect and reduce the positive charge on carbonyl carbon.
Ketones also have more crowding near carbonyl carbon than aldehydes.

notes.heading: Oxidation and Reduction of Aldehydes and Ketones
notes.text: Aldehydes and ketones undergo oxidation and reduction reactions. Aldehydes are easily oxidised, while ketones are difficult to oxidise.
points: Aldehydes contain the group –CHO and are easily oxidised to carboxylic acids.
General oxidation of aldehyde: RCHO —[O]→ RCOOH.
Example: CH3CHO —[O]→ CH3COOH. Ethanal gives ethanoic acid.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised because they have one hydrogen atom attached to the carbonyl carbon.
Common oxidising agents for aldehydes: KMnO4, K2Cr2O7/H+, HNO3, Tollens' reagent and Fehling's solution.
Tollens' reagent is [Ag(NH3)2]+, also called ammoniacal silver nitrate solution.
Aldehydes reduce Tollens' reagent to metallic silver and give silver mirror.
In Tollens' test, aldehyde is oxidised to carboxylate ion and Ag+ is reduced to Ag.
Example: CH3CHO —Tollens'→ CH3COO−.
Fehling's solution oxidises aliphatic aldehydes.
Fehling's test observation: red-brown precipitate of Cu2O.
Example: CH3CHO —Fehling's→ CH3COO−.
Copper(II) ion is reduced to cuprous oxide: Cu2+ → Cu2O.
Aromatic aldehydes such as benzaldehyde generally do not give Fehling's test.
Benzaldehyde gives Tollens' test but does not give Fehling's test.
Ketones contain R–CO–R' and do not have hydrogen attached to carbonyl carbon.
Ketones are difficult to oxidise.
Ketones do not react with mild oxidising agents such as Tollens' reagent and Fehling's solution.
Ketones generally do not give silver mirror test or Fehling's test.
Ketones can be oxidised only by strong oxidising agents under vigorous conditions.
Strong oxidation of ketones breaks carbon-carbon bonds near the carbonyl group and gives smaller carboxylic acids.
Example: CH3COCH3 —[O]→ CH3COOH + HCOOH.
Aldehydes are reduced to primary alcohols: RCHO —[H]→ RCH2OH.
Example: CH3CHO —[H]→ CH3CH2OH.
Ketones are reduced to secondary alcohols: RCOR' —[H]→ RCHOHR'.
Example: CH3COCH3 —[H]→ CH3CHOHCH3.
Common reducing agents: LiAlH4, NaBH4, H2/Ni, H2/Pt and H2/Pd.
Aldehydes and ketones can be completely reduced to hydrocarbons, changing >C=O into >CH2.
Clemmensen reduction uses Zn-Hg/HCl in acidic medium: R2C=O —Zn-Hg/HCl→ R2CH2.
Example: CH3COCH3 —Zn-Hg/HCl→ CH3CH2CH3.
Wolff-Kishner reduction uses NH2NH2/KOH and heat in basic medium: R2C=O —NH2NH2/KOH, Δ→ R2CH2.
Example: CH3COCH3 —NH2NH2/KOH, Δ→ CH3CH2CH3.
Difference: Clemmensen reduction is in acidic medium, while Wolff-Kishner reduction is in basic medium.
Important summary: aldehyde oxidation gives carboxylic acid; aldehyde reduction gives primary alcohol; ketone reduction gives secondary alcohol.
Memory trick: aldehyde has H, so it is easily oxidised; ketone has no H on carbonyl carbon, so it resists oxidation.

notes.heading: Tests to Distinguish Aldehydes and Ketones
notes.text: Aldehydes and ketones both contain the carbonyl group, but they can be distinguished because aldehydes are easily oxidised while ketones are not easily oxidised.
points: 2,4-DNP means 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine and is also called Brady's reagent.
Aldehydes and ketones both give 2,4-DNP test.
General 2,4-DNP reaction: R2C=O + H2NNHC6H3(NO2)2 → R2C=NNHC6H3(NO2)2 + H2O.
Product of 2,4-DNP test is 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone.
Observation in 2,4-DNP test: yellow, orange or red precipitate.
2,4-DNP test confirms carbonyl group but does not distinguish aldehydes from ketones.
Tollens' reagent is [Ag(NH3)2]+, called ammoniacal silver nitrate solution.
Aldehydes give Tollens' test and form silver mirror.
In Tollens' test, aldehyde is oxidised to carboxylate ion and Ag+ is reduced to Ag.
Ketones generally do not give Tollens' test because they are not easily oxidised.
Result of Tollens' test: aldehyde gives silver mirror; ketone gives no silver mirror.
Fehling's solution is an alkaline solution containing copper(II) ions and is blue in colour.
Aliphatic aldehydes give Fehling's test.
Fehling's test observation: red-brown precipitate of Cu2O.
Example: CH3CHO —Fehling's→ CH3COO−.
Ketones do not give Fehling's test.
Aromatic aldehydes such as benzaldehyde generally do not give Fehling's test.
Benzaldehyde gives Tollens' test but does not give Fehling's test.
Schiff's reagent is used to detect aldehydes.
Aldehydes restore pink or magenta colour of Schiff's reagent.
Ketones generally do not give Schiff's test.
Iodoform test is given by compounds containing CH3CO– group.
Iodoform test is also given by alcohols that can be oxidised to methyl ketones.
Reagent for iodoform test: I2/NaOH.
Observation in iodoform test: yellow precipitate of iodoform, CHI3, with characteristic smell.
Methyl ketones of general structure CH3COR give iodoform test.
Example: CH3COCH3 —I2/NaOH→ CHI3.
Among aldehydes, ethanal (CH3CHO) gives iodoform test.
Ethanal gives iodoform test because it forms the required CH3CO– condition under reaction conditions.
Iodoform test results: ethanal positive, methyl ketones positive, other aldehydes negative, non-methyl ketones negative.
Best tests to distinguish aldehydes and ketones are Tollens' test and Fehling's test.
Example: Ethanal and propanone can be distinguished by Tollens' reagent. Ethanal gives silver mirror, propanone does not.
Example: Benzaldehyde and acetophenone can be distinguished by Tollens' reagent. Benzaldehyde gives silver mirror, acetophenone does not.
Example: Acetaldehyde and acetone both give iodoform test, so use Tollens' test; acetaldehyde gives silver mirror and acetone does not.

Important NCERT points: aldehydes reduce Tollens' reagent; aldehydes reduce Fehling's solution; ketones generally do not reduce Tollens' or Fehling's reagent; 2,4-DNP is common test for aldehydes and ketones.

notes.heading: Preparation of Carboxylic Acids
notes.text: Carboxylic acids contain the functional group –COOH. They can be prepared by oxidation, hydrolysis and reaction of Grignard reagents with carbon dioxide.
points: General formula of carboxylic acids: R–COOH.
Examples: HCOOH is methanoic acid; CH3COOH is ethanoic acid; C6H5COOH is benzoic acid.
Primary alcohols on strong oxidation give carboxylic acids: RCH2OH —[O]→ RCOOH.
Example: CH3CH2OH —KMnO4/H+→ CH3COOH.
Primary alcohol first forms aldehyde, then aldehyde oxidises to carboxylic acid: RCH2OH → RCHO → RCOOH.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised to carboxylic acids: RCHO —[O]→ RCOOH.
Example: CH3CHO —KMnO4/H+→ CH3COOH.
Common oxidising agents: KMnO4, K2Cr2O7/H+, HNO3 and Tollens' reagent.
Aromatic carboxylic acids are prepared by oxidation of alkyl benzenes.
Toluene gives benzoic acid: C6H5CH3 —KMnO4/KOH, Δ→ C6H5COOH.
Any alkyl benzene having at least one benzylic hydrogen gives benzoic acid on oxidation.
Examples: C6H5CH3 → C6H5COOH and C6H5CH2CH3 → C6H5COOH.
Nitriles on hydrolysis give carboxylic acids: RCN —H2O/H+→ RCOOH.
In alkaline medium: RCN —OH−→ RCOO− —H+→ RCOOH.
Example: CH3CN —H2O/H+→ CH3COOH.
In nitriles, the carbon of CN becomes the carbon of COOH.
Amides on hydrolysis give carboxylic acids: RCONH2 —H2O/H+→ RCOOH.
Example: CH3CONH2 —H2O/H+→ CH3COOH.
In alkaline medium: RCONH2 —NaOH→ RCOONa —H+→ RCOOH.
Acid chlorides react with water to form carboxylic acids: RCOCl + H2O → RCOOH + HCl.
Example: CH3COCl + H2O → CH3COOH + HCl.
Acid anhydrides react with water to form carboxylic acids: (RCO)2O + H2O → 2RCOOH.
Example: (CH3CO)2O + H2O → 2CH3COOH.
Esters on hydrolysis give carboxylic acids and alcohols: RCOOR' + H2O —H+→ RCOOH + R'OH.
Example: CH3COOC2H5 + H2O —H+→ CH3COOH + C2H5OH.
Alkaline hydrolysis of esters gives salt of carboxylic acid and alcohol: RCOOR' + NaOH → RCOONa + R'OH.
Acidification gives carboxylic acid: RCOONa + HCl → RCOOH + NaCl.
Alkaline hydrolysis of esters is called saponification.
Grignard reagents react with carbon dioxide to form carboxylic acids after hydrolysis.
General reaction: RMgX + CO2 → RCOOMgX; RCOOMgX —H3O+→ RCOOH.
Example: CH3MgBr + CO2 —H3O+→ CH3COOH.
Grignard method increases the carbon chain by one carbon.
Important preparation of benzoic acid: C6H5CH3 —KMnO4/KOH, Δ→ C6H5COOH.
Memory trick: carboxylic acids are prepared mainly by oxidation and hydrolysis; Grignard reagent adds one carbon.

notes.heading: Physical Properties of Carboxylic Acids
notes.text: Carboxylic acids contain the carboxyl group, –COOH. They show special physical properties because they can form strong hydrogen bonds and dimers.
points: Lower carboxylic acids are generally liquids.
Higher carboxylic acids are solids.
Methanoic acid (HCOOH) is a liquid.
Ethanoic acid (CH3COOH) is a liquid.
Higher acids such as stearic acid (C17H35COOH) are solids.
Lower carboxylic acids have sharp and unpleasant smell.
Ethanoic acid has vinegar-like smell.
Higher carboxylic acids are almost odourless.
Carboxylic acids are polar compounds because the carboxyl group has C=O and O–H bonds.
Carboxylic acids form strong hydrogen bonds with each other.
Two molecules of carboxylic acid join together by hydrogen bonding. This is called dimer formation.
Dimer formation: 2RCOOH → (RCOOH)2.
Example: 2CH3COOH → (CH3COOH)2.
Dimer formation occurs especially in vapour state and in non-polar solvents.
Because of dimer formation, carboxylic acids have high boiling points.
Carboxylic acids have higher boiling points than aldehydes, ketones and alcohols of comparable molecular masses.
Boiling point order: carboxylic acids > alcohols > aldehydes/ketones > hydrocarbons.
Lower carboxylic acids are soluble in water.
Examples of water-soluble lower acids: HCOOH, CH3COOH and CH3CH2COOH.
Lower carboxylic acids dissolve in water because they form hydrogen bonds with water.
Solubility in water decreases as the carbon chain length increases.
The hydrocarbon part R– is non-polar and water-repelling.
Higher carboxylic acids are less soluble in water.
Carboxylic acids are soluble in organic solvents such as alcohol, ether and benzene.
Higher carboxylic acids are more soluble in organic solvents than in water due to their large non-polar hydrocarbon part.
Carboxylic acids have relatively high melting points due to strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
Even-numbered carboxylic acids generally have higher melting points than odd-numbered carboxylic acids because their molecules pack more closely in the crystal lattice.
Aldehydes and ketones can accept hydrogen bonds from water but cannot form strong hydrogen bonds with themselves.
Carboxylic acids can form hydrogen bonds with themselves and with water.

Important NCERT points: carboxylic acids have higher boiling points due to extensive hydrogen bonding and dimer formation; lower members are soluble in water; solubility decreases with molecular mass.

notes.heading: Chemical Reactions of Carboxylic Acids
notes.text: Carboxylic acids show reactions due to the acidic hydrogen of –COOH, the carbonyl group C=O and the hydroxyl group –OH.
points: Carboxylic acids behave as acids because they release H+ ion.
Ionisation: RCOOH ⇌ RCOO− + H+.
Example: CH3COOH ⇌ CH3COO− + H+.
Carboxylic acids react with active metals such as sodium to form salts and hydrogen gas.
With sodium: 2RCOOH + 2Na → 2RCOONa + H2.
Example: 2CH3COOH + 2Na → 2CH3COONa + H2.
Carboxylic acids react with bases to form salts and water.
With sodium hydroxide: RCOOH + NaOH → RCOONa + H2O.
Example: CH3COOH + NaOH → CH3COONa + H2O.
Carboxylic acids react with sodium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate to produce carbon dioxide gas.
With sodium carbonate: 2RCOOH + Na2CO3 → 2RCOONa + H2O + CO2.
With sodium bicarbonate: RCOOH + NaHCO3 → RCOONa + H2O + CO2.
Example: CH3COOH + NaHCO3 → CH3COONa + H2O + CO2.
Observation with NaHCO3: brisk effervescence of CO2.
Sodium bicarbonate test is used to identify carboxylic acids.
Esterification: carboxylic acids react with alcohols in presence of concentrated H2SO4 to form esters.
General esterification: RCOOH + R'OH —conc. H2SO4→ RCOOR' + H2O.
Example: CH3COOH + C2H5OH —conc. H2SO4→ CH3COOC2H5 + H2O.
Ethanoic acid and ethanol form ethyl ethanoate.
Esters usually have pleasant fruity smell.
Carboxylic acids react with thionyl chloride to form acid chlorides: RCOOH + SOCl2 → RCOCl + SO2 + HCl.
Example: CH3COOH + SOCl2 → CH3COCl + SO2 + HCl.
SOCl2 is preferred because by-products SO2 and HCl are gases and escape, giving pure acid chloride.
Carboxylic acids on heating with phosphorus pentoxide form acid anhydrides: 2RCOOH —P2O5→ (RCO)2O + H2O.
Example: 2CH3COOH —P2O5→ (CH3CO)2O + H2O.
Carboxylic acids react with ammonia to form ammonium carboxylate.
RCOOH + NH3 → RCOO−NH4+.
On heating, ammonium carboxylate gives amide: RCOO−NH4+ —Δ→ RCONH2 + H2O.
Example: CH3COOH + NH3 → CH3COO−NH4+; CH3COO−NH4+ —Δ→ CH3CONH2 + H2O.
Carboxylic acids can be reduced to primary alcohols using strong reducing agents.
Reduction: RCOOH —LiAlH4→ RCH2OH.
Example: CH3COOH —LiAlH4→ CH3CH2OH.
NaBH4 does not normally reduce carboxylic acids.
LiAlH4 is stronger and can reduce carboxylic acids.
Decarboxylation means removal of carbon dioxide.
Sodium salts of carboxylic acids on heating with soda lime give alkanes.
Soda lime is NaOH + CaO.
General decarboxylation: RCOONa + NaOH —CaO, Δ→ RH + Na2CO3.
Example: CH3COONa + NaOH —CaO, Δ→ CH4 + Na2CO3.
In decarboxylation, carbon chain decreases by one carbon.
Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction: carboxylic acids having alpha hydrogen react with chlorine or bromine in presence of red phosphorus to give alpha-halo carboxylic acids.
HVZ reaction: RCH2COOH —Br2/red P→ RCHBrCOOH.
Example: CH3COOH —Br2/red P→ BrCH2COOH.
In aromatic carboxylic acids, –COOH is electron withdrawing, deactivating and meta-directing.
Example: C6H5COOH —HNO3/H2SO4→ m-NO2C6H4COOH.
Benzoic acid gives mainly m-nitrobenzoic acid on nitration.

Important NCERT points: carboxylic acids give brisk effervescence with sodium bicarbonate due to CO2; carboxylic acids form esters with alcohols; sodium salts give alkanes with soda lime; –COOH is meta-directing.

notes.heading: Acidity of Carboxylic Acids
notes.text: Carboxylic acids are acidic because they can donate a proton, H+. Their acidity depends on the stability of the carboxylate ion formed after loss of H+.
points: The acidic group in carboxylic acids is –COOH.
Ionisation: RCOOH ⇌ RCOO− + H+.
Example: CH3COOH ⇌ CH3COO− + H+.
Carboxylic acids are acidic because they form a stable carboxylate ion, RCOO−, after losing H+.
The more stable the carboxylate ion, the stronger the acid.
Carboxylate ion is stabilised by resonance.
Resonance in carboxylate ion: R–C(=O)–O− ↔ R–C(–O−)=O.
The negative charge is delocalised over two oxygen atoms, making the ion stable.
Carboxylic acids are stronger acids than alcohols and phenols.
Acidity order: carboxylic acid > phenol > alcohol.
Carboxylate ion is more resonance-stabilised than alkoxide ion and phenoxide ion.
Electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity.
Examples of electron-withdrawing groups: –Cl, –Br, –NO2, –CN and –F.
Electron-withdrawing groups pull electron density away and stabilise the carboxylate ion.
Example: ClCH2COOH is stronger than CH3COOH due to the –I effect of chlorine.
More electron-withdrawing groups cause greater acidity.
Acidity order: CCl3COOH > CHCl2COOH > ClCH2COOH > CH3COOH.
Names: CCl3COOH is trichloroethanoic acid; CHCl2COOH is dichloroethanoic acid; ClCH2COOH is chloroethanoic acid; CH3COOH is ethanoic acid.
The effect of an electron-withdrawing group decreases with distance from –COOH.
Example: CH3CHClCOOH is stronger than ClCH2CH2COOH because chlorine is nearer to the carboxyl group.
Electron-donating groups decrease acidity.
Examples of electron-donating groups: –CH3 and –C2H5.
Electron-donating groups show +I effect and destabilise the carboxylate ion.
Methanoic acid is stronger than ethanoic acid: HCOOH > CH3COOH.
The methyl group in ethanoic acid shows +I effect and decreases acidity.
Acidity order of some aliphatic acids: HCOOH > CH3COOH > CH3CH2COOH.
As alkyl group size increases, +I effect increases slightly and acidity decreases.
Benzoic acid is C6H5COOH.
Benzoic acid is stronger than many aliphatic carboxylic acids because the phenyl group has electron-withdrawing effect.
Electron-withdrawing groups on benzene ring increase acidity of benzoic acid.
Examples: –NO2, –Cl and –CN increase acidity.
Nitrobenzoic acid is more acidic than benzoic acid: NO2C6H4COOH > C6H5COOH.
Electron-donating groups on benzene ring generally decrease acidity.
Examples: –CH3, –OCH3 and –OH generally decrease acidity.
Methylbenzoic acid is less acidic than benzoic acid: CH3C6H4COOH < C6H5COOH.
Ortho-substituted benzoic acids are usually stronger acids than benzoic acid, irrespective of whether the group is electron-donating or electron-withdrawing. This is called the ortho effect.
pKa is used to compare acidity.
Lower pKa means stronger acid; higher pKa means weaker acid.
Chloroethanoic acid has lower pKa than ethanoic acid, so it is stronger.
Carboxylic acids are stronger than phenols because carboxylate ion has negative charge delocalised over two electronegative oxygen atoms, while phenoxide ion is less stable.
Carboxylic acids react with sodium bicarbonate to produce carbon dioxide: RCOOH + NaHCO3 → RCOONa + H2O + CO2.
Observation in sodium bicarbonate test: brisk effervescence.
Phenols generally do not give sodium bicarbonate test because they are weaker acids.
Important rule: stable ion means stronger acid; electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity; electron-donating groups decrease acidity.

notes.heading: Important NCERT Reactions and Exam Revision Points
notes.text: This section collects important reactions, conversions and reasoning points from aldehydes, ketones and carboxylic acids for exam revision.
points: Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition because carbonyl carbon is partially positive: Cδ+=Oδ−.
General nucleophilic addition: R2C=O + Nu− → R2C(OH)Nu.
HCN addition gives cyanohydrins: R2C=O + HCN → R2C(OH)CN.
Example: CH3CHO + HCN → CH3CH(OH)CN.
Sodium hydrogen sulphite addition: RCHO + NaHSO3 → RCH(OH)SO3Na.
NaHSO3 gives crystalline addition products useful for purification and separation.
Grignard reagent RMgX gives alcohols after hydrolysis.
Formaldehyde + RMgX gives primary alcohol: HCHO + RMgX —H3O+→ RCH2OH.
Aldehyde + Grignard reagent gives secondary alcohol: RCHO + R'MgX —H3O+→ RCH(OH)R'.
Ketone + Grignard reagent gives tertiary alcohol: RCOR' + R''MgX —H3O+→ RC(OH)(R')R''.
With hydroxylamine: R2C=O + NH2OH → R2C=NOH + H2O; product is oxime.
With hydrazine: R2C=O + NH2NH2 → R2C=NNH2 + H2O; product is hydrazone.
With phenylhydrazine: R2C=O + C6H5NHNH2 → R2C=NNHC6H5 + H2O; product is phenylhydrazone.
With 2,4-DNP, aldehydes and ketones give yellow, orange or red precipitate of 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone.
Aldehyde reduction: RCHO —[H]→ RCH2OH.
Example: CH3CHO —LiAlH4→ CH3CH2OH.
Ketone reduction: RCOR' —[H]→ RCHOHR'.
Example: CH3COCH3 —NaBH4→ CH3CHOHCH3.
Clemmensen reduction uses Zn-Hg/HCl: R2C=O → R2CH2.
Example: CH3COCH3 → CH3CH2CH3.
Wolff-Kishner reduction uses NH2NH2/KOH and heat: R2C=O → R2CH2.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised to carboxylic acids: RCHO —[O]→ RCOOH.
Example: CH3CHO —KMnO4→ CH3COOH.
Tollens' test: aldehydes give silver mirror by reducing Ag+ to Ag.
Fehling's test: aliphatic aldehydes give red-brown precipitate of Cu2O.
Iodoform test is given by compounds containing CH3CO– group using I2/NaOH.
Iodoform test observation: yellow precipitate of CHI3.
Ethanal and propanone give iodoform test.
Aldol condensation is shown by aldehydes and ketones having alpha hydrogen.
Example: 2CH3CHO —dil. NaOH→ CH3CH(OH)CH2CHO.
On heating: CH3CH(OH)CH2CHO —Δ→ CH3CH=CHCHO + H2O.
The dehydration product is but-2-enal.
Cannizzaro reaction is shown by aldehydes that do not have alpha hydrogen in concentrated alkali.
Methanal Cannizzaro reaction: 2HCHO + NaOH → HCOONa + CH3OH.
Benzaldehyde Cannizzaro reaction: 2C6H5CHO + NaOH → C6H5COONa + C6H5CH2OH.
Carboxylic acids release H+: RCOOH ⇌ RCOO− + H+.
Carboxylic acids react with sodium bicarbonate: RCOOH + NaHCO3 → RCOONa + H2O + CO2.
Sodium bicarbonate test observation: brisk effervescence.
Esterification: RCOOH + R'OH —conc. H2SO4→ RCOOR' + H2O.
Example: CH3COOH + C2H5OH —conc. H2SO4→ CH3COOC2H5 + H2O.
Acid chloride formation: RCOOH + SOCl2 → RCOCl + SO2 + HCl.
SOCl2 is preferred because SO2 and HCl are gases and escape.
Amide formation: RCOOH + NH3 → RCOO−NH4+; on heating RCOO−NH4+ → RCONH2 + H2O.
Carboxylic acid reduction: RCOOH —LiAlH4→ RCH2OH.
Decarboxylation: RCOONa + NaOH —CaO, Δ→ RH + Na2CO3.
Example: CH3COONa + NaOH —CaO, Δ→ CH4 + Na2CO3.
HVZ reaction: RCH2COOH —Br2/red P→ RCHBrCOOH.
Example: CH3COOH —Br2/red P→ BrCH2COOH.
Rosenmund reduction: RCOCl —H2/Pd-BaSO4→ RCHO.
Stephen reaction: RCN —SnCl2/HCl→ RCH=NH —H2O→ RCHO.
Etard reaction: C6H5CH3 —CrO2Cl2→ C6H5CHO.
Gattermann-Koch reaction: C6H6 + CO + HCl —AlCl3/CuCl→ C6H5CHO.
Ketone from acid chloride: 2RCOCl + R'2Cd → 2RCOR' + CdCl2.
Ketone from nitrile: RCN + R'MgX —H3O+→ RCOR'.
Carboxylic acid from Grignard reagent: RMgX + CO2 —H3O+→ RCOOH.
The Grignard reagent with CO2 method increases the carbon chain by one carbon.
Conversion: ethanol to ethanoic acid: CH3CH2OH —KMnO4/H+→ CH3COOH.
Conversion: ethanol to ethanal: CH3CH2OH —PCC→ CH3CHO or CH3CH2OH —Cu, 573 K→ CH3CHO.
Conversion: ethanal to ethanol: CH3CHO —NaBH4→ CH3CH2OH.
Conversion: ethanal to ethanoic acid: CH3CHO —KMnO4→ CH3COOH.
Conversion: propanone to propan-2-ol: CH3COCH3 —NaBH4→ CH3CHOHCH3.
Conversion: propanone to propane: CH3COCH3 —Zn-Hg/HCl→ CH3CH2CH3 or CH3COCH3 —NH2NH2/KOH, Δ→ CH3CH2CH3.
Conversion: toluene to benzoic acid: C6H5CH3 —KMnO4/KOH, Δ→ C6H5COOH.
Conversion: benzoyl chloride to benzaldehyde: C6H5COCl —H2/Pd-BaSO4→ C6H5CHO.
Aldehyde and ketone can be distinguished by Tollens' reagent: aldehyde gives silver mirror; ketone gives no silver mirror.
Ethanal and propanone both give iodoform test, so Tollens' reagent is used to distinguish them.
Ethanoic acid and phenol can be distinguished by sodium bicarbonate: ethanoic acid gives CO2 effervescence, phenol does not.
Benzaldehyde and benzoic acid can be distinguished by sodium bicarbonate: benzoic acid gives CO2 effervescence, benzaldehyde does not.
Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones because they have less steric hindrance, less +I effect and a more positive carbonyl carbon.
Aldehydes give Tollens' test but ketones do not because aldehydes are easily oxidised to acids, while ketones are not easily oxidised.
Carboxylic acids are stronger than phenols because carboxylate ion is stabilised by resonance over two oxygen atoms.
ClCH2COOH is more acidic than CH3COOH because chlorine shows –I effect and stabilises the carboxylate ion.
